title: Cat Paw Pads Peeling: Causes, Care, and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat's paw pad peeling? Learn about common causes, when to call the vet, and how to triage this low-urgency condition at home.
content_html:
TL;DR: Peeling paw pads in cats are usually caused by environmental irritants or underlying skin conditions and are rarely an emergency unless accompanied by bleeding or lethargy. Clear photos and a veterinary consultation are recommended to identify the cause and prevent infection.

What causes peeling paw pads in cats?

Peeling paw pads, or pad sloughing, occurs when the tough, protective layer of skin on your cat's feet becomes compromised. Common causes include:

  • Environmental dryness
  • Contact with household irritants
  • Walking on surfaces that are too hot or cold
  • Immune-mediated conditions like Plasma Cell Pododermatitis (commonly known as "pillow foot")

Is my cat’s peeling paw pad a medical emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. Generally, peeling paw pads are not a medical emergency unless accompanied by:

  • Heavy bleeding
  • An inability to walk
  • Signs of systemic illness, such as lethargy and loss of appetite

While not usually an emergency, it is often a sign of discomfort that requires a scheduled veterinary consultation to prevent secondary infections.

How can a photo help a veterinarian triage my cat's paw issue?

Providing a high-resolution photo of the affected paw is essential for remote triage. A clear image allows a veterinary professional to assess:

  • The color of the tissue
  • The depth of the peeling
  • The presence of any discharge or swelling

This visual information is critical in determining whether the issue is a simple case of dry skin or a condition requiring prescription medication.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hyperkeratosis and ulceration of the footpads can be a cutaneous manifestation of systemic disease, particularly in cases of feline skin fragility syndrome associated with pancreatic or hepatic neoplasia, hepatic lipidosis, or adrenal dysfunction. Additionally, superficial perivascular to lichenoid dermatitis with marked diffuse parakeratotic hyperkeratosis and striking inter- and intracellular edema limited to the upper half of the epidermis can cause footpad lesions. A thorough dermatologic examination is critical, documenting the type and distribution of lesions to identify the underlying cause.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 525)

faqs.question: Can I use human moisturizer on my cat's paws?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid human lotions. Many contain chemicals, fragrances, or ingredients like zinc that can be toxic if your cat licks them. Only use balms specifically labeled as pet-safe.

faqs.question: Could my cat have burned their paws?
faqs.answer: Yes, walking on hot pavement or a hot stovetop is a common cause of sudden peeling and blistering. If you suspect a thermal burn, seek veterinary care to manage pain and prevent infection.

faqs.question: What are the signs of an infected paw pad?
faqs.answer: Watch for redness, warmth, a foul odor, or yellow/green discharge. If your cat is excessively licking the area or limping, an infection may be present.
